one_line_takeaway: State Backdoor shows that a robot's initial joint-state configuration -- rather than a visible image trigger -- can serve as a highly effective (>90% ASR), functionality-preserving, and defense-evasive backdoor trigger for VLA models in real-world settings, and that the same mechanism doubles as a dataset watermarking tool.
key_finding: State Backdoor achieves a 90%+ average ASR across models/tasks (e.g., ACT 98.3% ASR on Pick-and-Place, pi0 96.1%, OpenVLA 95.2%) while keeping SR close to the clean 'None' baseline (e.g., ACT SR 78.6% vs clean 80.4%), consistently outperforming both TrojanRobot (ASR ~35-71% across tasks/models) and BadVLA (ASR ~54-77%). On LIBERO simulation the gap narrows, and on the 'Long' task suite all methods including State Backdoor show markedly lower ASR/SR (e.g., State Backdoor ASR 43.2-90.1% depending on model, vs None SR as low as 49.8-52.3%), indicating the trigger's advantage is real-world-specific and diminishes on harder/longer simulated tasks. PGA converges faster than PSO, GA, and Grid-search (e.g., 2.12h/90% ASR on Pick vs PSO 3.53h/89%, GA 5.22h/86%, Grid-search 6.33h/85%) while matching or exceeding their ASR. The Opposite Action Trajectory labeling substantially outperforms random-trajectory labeling on SmolVLA (e.g., Pick-and-Place ASR 91.4% vs 75.6%, SR 92.1% vs 79.3%). Against defenses, State Backdoor survives Fine-pruning (ASR stays above 90% across pruning ratios), image compression (ASR remains 86% even at 50% quality), and Semantic Shield (average ASR drops only from 90.0% to 89.7%), because the trigger lives in the state space rather than the visual/pixel space that these defenses target. The watermarking application achieves 97.3% average Top-1 and 99.7% average Top-10 keys accuracy with clean validation accuracy of 86.9% and log10(p) > 14 (k=1) / > 18 (k=10), but the watermark signal degrades under extensive (though not light) fine-tuning.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'State Backdoor,' a poisoning-based backdoor attack against Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robotic control models that uses the robot arm's initial joint-state vector (a 6-DoF configuration s0 in R^6) as the backdoor trigger, instead of a visible object inserted into the camera image as in prior work. The authors design a Preference-guided Genetic Algorithm (PGA), a gradient-free, black-box search over the state space that optimizes a triggered state t* by jointly maximizing attack effectiveness (loss of a surrogate model on poisoned samples), preserving clean-task performance, and minimizing the L2 magnitude of the state perturbation (via a soft-constraint penalty term) to keep the trigger state physically/visually plausible. They also introduce 'Opposite Action Trajectory' labeling: instead of assigning poisoned samples random failure trajectories (which they show creates high-variance outlier samples the model cannot learn), they negate each action component of the clean trajectory (a_fail = -a1,...,-aT) to produce a structurally consistent poisoned label distribution. Beyond the attack, the paper proposes and evaluates a secondary application: using the same state-trigger mechanism as a dataset watermarking technique for VLA training data (ownership verification), evaluated with Validation Accuracy, Top-k Keys Accuracy, and a binomial-test log10(p) significance metric.
threat_model: Attacker performs black-box data poisoning at the fine-tuning stage using real-world collected data (not simulation, unlike prior work). The attacker has access only to the training dataset and no knowledge of the victim VLA model's architecture, parameters, or training pipeline; they cannot alter the training process. To craft poisoned data, the attacker is allowed to use a surrogate model (an approximation of the victim) for scoring trigger candidates. At inference time the attacker can only perturb the robot's initial joint state, not the camera image or task instruction. The paper justifies the initial-state manipulation assumption by analogy to legitimate maintenance/calibration technicians who routinely reset robot joint configurations, and cites prior RL/robotic-safety literature that treats the initial state as a controllable perturbation surface. Attack goals stated explicitly: functionality-preserving (minimal impact on clean performance), effectiveness (untargeted task failure on trigger), and stealthiness (inconspicuous to human inspection).
limitations: The paper's own text acknowledges: (1) the watermark/backdoor signal weakens under extensive fine-tuning, though it survives light fine-tuning (Fig. 12, section VII-B); (2) on the LIBERO 'Long' task suite, both clean baselines and all attacks (including State Backdoor) show substantially degraded SR/ASR, suggesting reduced attack reliability on longer-horizon tasks; (3) no existing defense is specifically designed for VLAs, so the three defenses tested (Fine-pruning, image compression, Semantic Shield) are adaptations of vision/classification-domain defenses rather than VLA-native ones, and the paper explicitly states 'the design of effective defense mechanisms against backdoor attacks in VLA models remains an important direction for future research' -- i.e., the authors do not claim to have found or tested a defense that actually works against their attack. (4) The threat model requires physical/administrative access to set the robot's initial joint configuration (justified via a maintenance-technician analogy), which is a real but non-trivial precondition compared to purely digital data-poisoning threat models.

datasets_used: Five real-world manipulation datasets collected on the SO-101 6-DoF robotic arm: Pick-and-Place, Drawer Opening, Button Pressing, Peg Insertion, Tennis Pushing (100 real-world samples each, video + instruction + 6-DoF state sequence)
LIBERO simulation benchmark (Spatial, Object, Goal, Long task suites)
quantitative_results: ASR 90%+ average across five VLA models and five real-world tasks (paper's headline claim)
Pick-and-Place ASR: ACT 98.3%, DP 93.1%, SmolVLA 91.7%, pi0 96.1%, OpenVLA 95.2% (Table III)
PGA time/ASR on Pick-and-Place: 2.12h/90% vs PSO 3.53h/89%, GA 5.22h/86%, Grid-search 6.33h/85% (Table V)
Fine-pruning defense: State Backdoor ASR stays above 90% across all pruning ratios 0-10% (Fig. 10)
Image compression defense: ASR remains 86% even at 50% image quality (Fig. 11)
Semantic Shield defense: average ASR drops only from 90.0% to 89.7%, SR from 85.5% to 85.3% (Table VIII)
Opposite vs Random trajectory labeling on SmolVLA, Pick-and-Place: ASR 91.4% (opposite) vs 75.6% (random); SR 92.1% vs 79.3% (Table VI)
Watermarking: average Top-1 keys accuracy 97.3%, Top-10 99.7%, validation accuracy 86.9%, log10 p > 14 (k=1) / > 18 (k=10) (Table IX)
Surrogate model transferability: ASR/SR vary by less than 5% across five different surrogate models (Table VII)
LIBERO 'Long' suite: State Backdoor ASR ranges 43.2-90.1% depending on model, versus None-baseline clean SR as low as 49.8-52.3% (Table IV), showing degraded reliability on long-horizon tasks
baselines_compared: TrojanRobot (prior SOTA VLA backdoor attack, visible physical trigger, white-box)
BadVLA (prior SOTA VLA backdoor attack, objective-decoupled optimization, white-box, simulation-trained)
PSO, GA, Grid-search (trigger-search optimization baselines compared against PGA)
